speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. I'm Leslie [? Fass. ?] Mr. Zelikson, you were discussing being lost after watching the massacre by the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct

speaker: interviewer
text: Of many people who were from the Dvinsk ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dvinsk ghetto, and from the other-- [? Reznich ?] and some other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of, a lot of people came from [? Reznich. ?] It's outside a big city. And-- some of them from Riga they brought over, all the Jews they could get a hold of, they brought in Dvinsk ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this massacre in December, '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or after that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened to you next, after you were separate-- separated from your mother? Then I walked in the forest. I didn't know what to do. And I started to go-- instead to go the right way, I went the wrong way. And then I saw a big, gigantic tree. And I looked at the tree. And then the tree opened up and somebody grabbed me by the arm and pulled me in, in the tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw six people. And there was a German sitting like you're sitting next to me. He was sitting. And one guy came out. It was five young people. And one was the older man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the German tied up?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was tied up, his hands, yes-- behind his back. And he come over and says, you want to join us? I say, yes, I want to join us. But I had lost my mother. My mother is-- I was returned forest. And I explained to him what happened, that we saw the people get killed. He says forget about your mother. Because either she's going to get caught or we'll find her. But right now, forget about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but I want to see my mother. I'm all by myself. He said, you want to stay with us? I say, yes. He say, you see this man? And he took out the gun. He says, you kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put back the gun. I says, I don't nothing about guns. I don't know how to handle a gun. I never handled. I'm a child. He says, in order to stay with us, we have to trust you. In order to trust, you have to kill him. And he give me the gun. And he, he takes the gun and pulls the-- excuse me-- the clutch. And he says, it's all loaded. Hold it with both hands and just pull the, the trigger. And I told him, I can't do it. It's against my religion. And I never shot nobody. I can't do it. He took out another gun and put it against my head. And he says, either you pull the trigger, or I'll pull my trigger. I had no choice. And I pulled the trigger. An all the blood came running-- my face, and my-- all over. And the man fell. The head fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cry then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I hit him right in the head. He didn't even blink. He j-- his head just snapped. And he says, [RUSSIAN SPEECH]. It means in Russian that you're a good boy. And he says, for now on, you're with us. And they showed me then how to use a gun and knife. And then he says, you have to bring a prisoner. But you can't have a knife. You can't have a gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, how I'm going to bring a prisoner? Then he gave a piece piano wire. It had two loops. And you put it in between the two middle fingers. And if you see them, you just go like this. And if you don't tighten it too much, you won't chop his head off, you'll just hurt him. And he'll go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we had to bring in prisoners. We caught prisoners. But a couple of times, I went out. It was OK. And then I got caught again. And the German caught me and put me to-- back to the ghetto, the Dvinsk ghetto. And I told them that I was working for this guy, but the obersturmfuhrer, he moved out. They brought in a, a new, new guy. So the new guy didn't know that I escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Dvinsk ghetto look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like barracks, like soldiers' living quarters used to be, the Latvian soldiers' living quarter. So they moved out. And they made the people move there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a barrier around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a wall of barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: With dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else? What else could you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was German Shepherd dogs, Doberman Pinscher dogs, and big black dogs that were there. They, they all say [? hank ?]. eight people-- yeah, that I'm coming to it. There was a German officer. And he fell in love with a Jewish girl. And he begged the Jewish girl to change to German so he could save her. And she says she'll never relinquish her Jewish inheritance. And he says, if you don't go with me, I'll kill myself. And she thought he was joking. He took out a gun. And he shot himself. The German find it out, the Nazis. They brought everybody to attention again. And they says, we're going to take 10 people. Every number five goes out. And they s-- and they start to count one, two, three, four, five, out. One, two, three, four, five, out. And they took out 10 people. I was number four, thank God. And they took them. And they brought them over. They build a platform. And they put them on the platform. And they hang them. And let them hang for three days. And everybody had to go around in a circle to see. And they put a sign on his chest, on each one of them. Some of them was very young, some of them old. And they say they all-- they killed the German soldier. They're responsible for the German soldier. They did not meet the German soldier who killed himself. So they say the-- whoever is responsible and don't come off, they'll kill 10 if you don't come forward. Of course, nobody could come forward. Because he killed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the German soldier's name, who killed himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zelikson, you were describing the hanging.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the German soldier who shot himself or of his Jewish lover?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish lover was Chana. But the German soldier, I don't remember. He was a young-- an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were describing that they put placards on every fifth--

speaker: interviewee
text: After they hang them, they put on the chest a piece big paper with letters that they killed the German soldier. And that's what they were punished for. But they were innocent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took my-- yeah, at that time, I met my mother again. They caught her when she come to Dvinsk ghetto. But then buses came. There were three buses. And they said they're going to transfer the people to a different camp-- the people that were on the left side, not on the right side. They put me on the right side. My mother, they put on the left side, which I didn't know. And then I heard all the people that go-- went on the left, they put them in the buses. And I thought they're moving them to a different camp. But the people on the right says, they're going to kill them. I, I ran out. I wanted to go. But the I got hit with a-- not a German, but a Latvian, Isaac. He hit me with a, with a stick. I should go back. Which, I went back. I had no choice. And in the buses, they had gas. While they were driving, the driver had the gas mask. I find that out after that. And they were all gassed in the buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And us, they send us to-- the, the soldiers from the front, the uniform was coming in. So we had to clean out the uniform, take out the wallets, so whatever they had, the belongings. And a lot of times, we saw a hand, part of a leg in the uniform, stuck to it. We had to remove that. And we took out the wallets, cigarettes, pipes, from the uniform. That lasted maybe-- by the January.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were cleaning out the remains of the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- right. They was sh--shipping it from Russia, the German soldiers. And-- but in January, they send us to Kaiserwald. They took trucks. And they liquidated the Dvinsk ghetto. There was no more people in  the Dvinsk ghetto. They took everybody out and send us to Kaiserwald. That was in Riga, outskirt of Riga

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in January?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a friend or somebody that sustained you during all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from Riga ghetto-- from Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not, not from the Dvinsk ghetto to the Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, the Dvinsk ghetto, I had cousins which I find out that will come later in Israel. They were in Dvinsk ghetto with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're still in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I say I'll come to that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after your mother was shot, was that why you, you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother wasn't shot. My mother was gassed in the bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother was gassed in the bus, where were you at that time? In which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Dvinsk ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dvinsk ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friendships with anyone who supported you through all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a couple of friends. We were together. But we got separate in Kaiserwald. And I don't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how soon after your mother was gassed in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next month.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next month.

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us to Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what was your activity? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dvinsk ghetto, after your mother died. What type of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back and did the, the housework. And I used to light up the fire for the German guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all your family by then was gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So continuing-- you were shipped to Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaiserwald-- that was a slave labor camp, also extermination camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember-- you arrive there by truck or by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Army trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who you were with when you got off the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went-- had so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone that was your-- a friend? Anyone who became close to you from the Dvinsk ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Achem, ?] but we got separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like getting off the truck? What were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no time to think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting off the truck, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They pushed us, they pushed us. And they had dogs. And if you weren't fast enough, they hit you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you entered--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to-- you had to run to, to the barracks. And when we came there, we saw a lot of people, much more people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wearing their, their clothing, except a yellow star-- a Jewish star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a yellow star from the Dvinsk ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were already in the Kaiserwald uniform, so to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what was it like? You got off the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went off the truck. They brought us in the barracks. They separate-- the young people separate, the old people separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to describe that more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told us to go to these barracks. And all the people to go to the outer barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a direction, left or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us, we're going to right. They go left. And the people that went to the right, they selected to do some slave-- excuse me-- slave labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to undress and be examined?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go and to take off our clothes. And they put us in, in a, a, a showers, a big place were you we take all our clothes off and put us in the showers. To wash [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to wash up, that we would be clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shave your heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they shaved our heads. And they left us, they left us with the clothes. They told us we're going-- they took us with trucks. We're going to go to work. We had to work to build streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a uniform after you took off your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Kaiserwald-- when we got in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took off your clothes, you showered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they shaved your heads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you put--

speaker: interviewee
text: They shaved the head actually before we had the shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't remember. To be honest with you, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whose clothes did you put on after the shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: We put on their own clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you were lucky, you had shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if you found your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, I had sandals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they your sandals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandals, yes-- my sandals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the shower and you got dressed, where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us to work. That was early in the morning. They took us building streets, doing farm work to pick potatoes, take out carrots. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lettuce. In Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the public people see you in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- only the farmer when we went to the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: They saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw. They saw these soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Latvian farmers saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their reaction to the, to the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reaction. They laughed that we were in-- like prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any conversation? Did anyone call out anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dirty, dirty Jews, the used to call us-- lazy, dirty Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number when you went to Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did-- 175.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was on a garment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right by the chest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken to the farm areas to pluck carrots and potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah-- mostly build streets, cut stones, chop wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of food did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us-- soup was fish soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the, like, cut fish soup. It smells terrible. And if we worked hard, then they give us a small piece of bread and a raw potato.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you lost a lot of weight by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was a skeleton. I didn't weigh myself, but I know I, I, I couldn't weigh no more than 90 pound.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember how tall you were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly the-- my height. I'm now 5' 9'. But at that time, I was young. I couldn't been no more than maybe 5' 5', something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you weighed 90 pounds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked in the farm area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farm, or the street, or in the forest, chopping wood-- and that lasted only six months. And then they send us to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason they sent you to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give us any reason. We finished over there. And they send us someplace else. In Stutthof, we took all our garment, everything, all our possession, whatever we had, we had to leave there. And they put a stripe clothes on with the big Jewish star with the number. My number was 238.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a hat, it was like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cap-- the same, the same as the garment-- stripe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you arrived from Kaiserwald to Stutthof? How were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us by train, freight train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did your other survivors think that they were going to be killed at that point? Where did you think you were being sent? Well, we didn't know. We didn't know, because we didn't even figure that we're going to survive. Sooner of later, we didn't think of surviving. We thought when we're going to get killed. That, that was actually our expression. Because we didn't mean anything but surviving. We were survivors. We tried to survive. But in our mind, we know we're going to get killed sooner or later. Did anyone try to escape from the train from Kaiserwald to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they tried in Stutthof. And they caught them. And they, they, they feed them to the dogs-- two people in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: People you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac was one.

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Berkowitz was one. And there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was-- Chaim, Chaim Liebowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason they were shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried to escape. No, they weren't shot. They've wished they would shot-- be shot. They caught them. And they had maybe 50, 60, dogs there. And they fed them to the dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: They fed them alive to the dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they put them in a circle. And they sic the dogs on them. And the dogs rip them apart, limb by limb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced to watch, or could you turn away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, we have to stay to attention. We had to stay to attention. And if you turned your head, you got hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a circle around this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to stay in, in attention, like an appell, they used to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In rows?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, in rows-- maybe about 12 in one line. And we had to watch. Everybody had to watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe an hour, hour, maybe an hour and a half. I didn't count. I didn't think. But we had to stay and watch. And the dogs was ripping them apart. In beginning, there was screaming, And then it was quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical day like for you in Stutthof, in the morning to the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very light. Was very light. We used to go out work 6:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: The appell-- every time that you came out before you work, you had to stay attention. They count the heads. Actually, what they counted is the numbers in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to call out your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called your name-- not your name, the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your number-- you had no name,

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your work that was so light?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your work. The work you did at Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you I had to do. I had to chop wood. I had to cut stones. I had to do the street. And when I was lucky, they send me to the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any acts of kindness between the German soldiers and the inmates at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or when you were sent out to the farm areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You-- if you said anything or you looked at him, you get beat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: We took-- they were carrying sticks, rubber sticks. And they will hit you plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were very thin at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, I was about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you entered around January '41-- '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I went-- they send us to Neuengamme. From Stutthof to Neuengamme, they sent us by trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone in Stutthof who was a prominent member of the community or a famous person that you recognized?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone there who, who was able to mark the Jewish calendar? Did you know what month it was, what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh you know, one thing I forgotten with Kaiserwald. In Kaiserwald, there was a lot of rabbis. We did long prayers with the [INAUDIBLE], with tzitzits. And the German officers called them all out and told them to take off the [INAUDIBLE]. And they give them a shear to cut the payots off. But they say it's against their religion. They wouldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, that's the last warning I'm giving you. Either you do it, or we're going to take it. But they didn't do it. So the German put gasoline-- poured gasoline on them. And they put a match by the payots and burn the payots.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rabbis started to-- they weren't cry. But they were saying the Bible. They took out a Bible. And they started to say the Bible. So the German says, pour the whole gasoline on them and burn them, and which they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- that was in Kaiserwald. Also, in Dvinsk ghetto-- also, in Dvinsk ghetto that I, I forgot to mention, they put a lot of Jews in the synagogue. And they told them-- they took cameras. And they told them going to take pictures and send it to the other Jews in Germany, show them how the Jews pray. And everybody went in the synagogue. And then they locked the synagogue up and put gasoline on the synagogue and burned the synagogue and all the Jews in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
